title: Practical Log-Depth Quantum State Preparation and Circuit Verification via Tree Tensor Network Compilation
abstract: Matrix product states provide efficient classical descriptions of quantum systems that may be useful as reference states for quantum algorithms such as quantum phase estimation and quantum-selected configuration interaction. Shallow circuit constructions for loading matrix product states onto quantum computers is necessary for this to be practical on near-term hardware. We present a decomposition of matrix product states to log-depth quantum circuits via a simple tree tensor network renormalisation procedure. Our method exposes an explicit parameter which can be used to trade a small amount of fidelity for large savings in circuit depth. We extend this decomposition to the case of matrix product operators allowing us to construct log-depth and ancilla-free circuits to calculate overlaps of the form $\left |\langleφ|U|ψ\rangle\right |^2$. In particular, we demonstrate an interpretation of these circuits as \emph{verifier circuits} with application to circuit-level device calibration.
summary: This paper introduces an analytical method to convert Matrix Product States into quantum circuits with logarithmic depth instead of linear depth. By using a tree tensor network renormalization approach, it allows for much shallower circuits that are more resilient to noise on near-term quantum hardware. The method also extends to verifying unitary operations using Matrix Product Operators.
main_problem: The high circuit depth of traditional linear-depth (staircase) mappings for loading Matrix Product States (MPS) onto quantum computers makes them impractical for noisy, near-term hardware.
main_result: The authors developed an analytical method to decompose MPS and MPO into log-depth quantum circuits using Tree Tensor Network (TTN) renormalization, enabling efficient state preparation and circuit verification.
method: A Tree Tensor Network renormalization procedure uses iterative local merges and Singular Value Decompositions (SVD) to transform MPS into a binary tree structure, which is then mapped to quantum gates.
model_or_system: The method focuses on N-qubit systems represented by Matrix Product States (MPS) and Matrix Product Operators (MPO) with a bond dimension chi.
key_observables: Circuit depth, fidelity (overlap), gate locality, and the squared overlap magnitude |<phi|U|psi>|^2 for verifier circuits.
important_parameters: Circuit depth scaling (O(log N) vs O(N)), bond dimension (chi), system size (N), and noise parameter (delta).
main_assumptions: The method assumes the MPS can be padded to a bond dimension of 2^k and that the unitary U has an efficient MPO representation; it also assumes a trade-off between fidelity and depth via truncation.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate tensor ranks, the structural difference between MPS and MPO, the linear depth of staircase circuits versus the log-depth of TTN, the SVD renormalization process, and performance metrics like fidelity decay and gate size saturation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of MPS loading, presents the TTN renormalization method, provides an error analysis for truncation, extends the method to MPOs for circuit verification, and demonstrates utility for device calibration.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Basic tensors depicted in the tensor network graphical language. A tensor is represented by a node with a number of legs equal to the tensor rank.
FIG. 2: A matrix product state (MPS) and a matrix product operator (MPO), each with 4 sites.
FIG. 3: An MPS with N = 6 and χ = 4 in canonical form is mapped directly to a linear-depth staircase circuit with maximum gate size 1 + log (χ).
FIG. 4: A 3-layer TTN with χ = 2 and orthogonality centre at the root is mapped directly to a log-depth staircase circuit.
FIG. 5: A series of local site merges and singular value decompositions brings an MPS into the form of a TTN.
